channel.title: DutchNews.nl
channel.description: DutchNews.nl brings daily news from The Netherlands in English

item.title: Court documents riddled with wrong names, say whistleblowers
item.description:
Court documents riddled with wrong names, say whistleblowers

February 23, 2026

Court verdicts continue to be riddled with illegally and wrongly “corrected” names, causing innocent people to go to prison and others to evade punishment, whistleblowers at the judicial information service Justid told the Telegraaf.

Justid is in charge of collecting, managing and linking confidential information about identity, records, residence status and detention information. The service also manages the identity database SKDB.

Justid workers often change names by hand if they spot what they think is a mistake, the whistleblowers said.  According to one anonymous worker, “a name pops up and you check: is he in the system? You think: it must be him”.

Changing names, incorrectly or not at all, can have serious consequences, the audit office concluded last year, lumbering innocent people with a conviction while others evade punishment.

Last year, the audit office said Justid found 876 mistaken identities in verdicts, 141 of which were linked to serious crimes such as murder and manslaughter, terrorism and crimes related to drugs and sexual assault.

“This is the tip of the iceberg,” whistleblower Marleen de Wilde told the Telegraaf. One of her colleagues, who wished to remain anonymous, said the number could be as high as 50,000.

“Many cases don’t see the light of day,” De Wilde said. “Convicted criminals who never go to prison will keep quiet, of course.

“But if you are innocent, you will only find out when your name comes up in connection with something else,” she said, for instance, when requesting a certificate of good conduct (VOG).

De Wilde said one reason the problem is allowed to continue is “a culture of fear” at Justid, which is stopping workers from flagging up mistakes.
item.dc:creator: Hanneke Sanou

item.title: Environmental permit could scupper Lelystad airport opening
item.description:
Environmental permit could scupper Lelystad airport opening

February 23, 2026

The new cabinet wants to open Lelystad airport to holiday flights, but experts have serious doubts about whether it will be granted an environmental licence, news website Nu.nl reported on Monday.

Previous administrations have invested €243 million in preparing Lelystad for commercial operations, but the airport still needs an official permit because of the impact on the surrounding countryside.

The Schiphol group, the state-owned owner of Lelystad airport, is preparing to launch commercial flights from 2027, but experts question whether that timetable is feasible.

Nitrogen emissions are expected to rise within a 25-kilometre radius of the airport and nearby nature reserves are already in poor condition due to high nitrogen levels that first need to be addressed, environmental policy professor Raoul Beunen told Nu.nl.

Lelystad is currently used by private aircraft and a flying school. Many charter airlines have also expressed resistance to being relocated to Lelystad airport, which lacks a direct link to Schiphol and has no train station.

The airport is also set to become a base for F-35 fighter jets, a plan that is likely to proceed on national security grounds.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pascoe

item.title: The Dutch are becoming less trusting of others, survey shows
item.description:
The Dutch are becoming less trusting of others, survey shows

February 23, 2026

The Dutch are generally happy with their lives but are becoming less likely to trust other people, according to new research by the government’s socio-cultural think tank the SCP.

In the three years since the last survey, the number of people saying most people are to be trusted has fallen from 69% to 61%, the SCP said. The research, not yet published but seen by the NRC, will be used by the government when drawing up its spring financial statement.

The downturn is not a new development, senior researcher Roel Willems told the paper, but the divide along educational lines is. Just 47% of people with little or no academic post-school education say most people are to be trusted, compared with 73% of those with a degree.

The difference is also reflected in income, with 50% of low-income people showing a high level of trust in others, compared with 68% of high earners.

The gap, Willems said, is down to a number of factors. “These people often live in poorer neighbourhoods and are less positive about life in general,” he said. “That also contributes to being less trusting.” More than half of those questioned with a low income felt connected to where they lived, compared with almost two-thirds of higher earners.

The Netherlands scores well compared with other European countries, coming behind Norway, Iceland and Finland in terms of trusting others. The Dutch are also more likely than most others to say they are happy with their lives. Some 87% give their lives a score of at least seven out of 10, in line with Switzerland.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pascoe

item.title: Dutch Olympic committee hopes for funding boost after gold rush
item.description:
Dutch Olympic committee hopes for funding boost after gold rush

February 23, 2026

The Dutch Olympic association has said the new government needs to keep up funding for sport if the country is to build on its record medal haul in Milan.

The team ended the winter games with 20 medals, including 10 golds, and an unprecedented third place in the medal table behind Norway and the United States.

André Cats, sporting director of the Olympic association NOC*NSF, told a press conference at the close of the games that he hoped the incoming cabinet would “have our backs”.

“I don’t want to make this a political tirade or start banging on about extra budgets, because the performances and medals have to take precedence,” he said.

“But if you look at the medals we won at the summer games in Paris [where the Netherlands took 15 medals two years ago] and here, we’re under pressure in budget terms.”

The previous cabinet had plans to cut €28 million a year from the sports budget by scrapping subsidies to develop and maintain sports facilities. However, it backtracked on a plan to raise VAT for tickets and membership fees from 9% to 21%.

Councils increased spending on sport by an average of 2.8% in 2024, despite financial pressures in local government, research based on figures from the statistics agency CBS found.

Carl Verheijen, chef de mission of the Dutch team in Milan, said: “Finishing third in the medal table behind Norway and the US is fantastic for our small country.

“We’ve made huge strides, particularly in short track, where we got five golds when we were expecting two or three.”

Broader appeal

The Netherlands finished with 10 gold medals, seven silver and three bronze, just ahead of host nation Italy, who won 10 gold and six silver medals. Italy, France, Germany, Switzerland and Japan all won more medals overall than the Dutch.

Cats said NOC*NSF had ambitions to build on the success by investing in developing downhill sports such as snowboarding and bobsled, as well as the curling team, which narrowly missed out on qualifying for its first Olympics.

Kimberley Bos was unable to repeat her historic bronze medal at skeleton bob in Beijing, finishing 13th, while snowboarders Melissa Peperkamp and Romy van Vreden missed out on the final.

“We didn’t get a top eight finish, which was less than we’d hoped for. So we’re going to look at where we can do better. It’s how we measure every sport,” Cats said.

All skating medals

All the Dutch medals in Milan were won in speed skating or short track speed skating, with the short-rink version delivering 50% of the Dutch gold medals for the first time.

Short track shaker Jens van ’t Wout won two individual golds and another in the relay, joining a select band of four Dutch athletes who have won three events at a single Olympics.

Xandra Velzeboer won two gold medals in the women’s shorttrack, while the women won four events on the long track, with Femke Kok, Jutta Leerdam and Antoinette Rijpsma-De Jong sweeping the sprints.

On the final day Jorrit Bergsma and Marijke Groenewoud won the men’s and women’s mass start races. Bergsma, who also took a bronze medal in the 10,000m, is the oldest ever Dutch Olympic medallist at the age of 40.
item.dc:creator: Gordon Darroch

item.title: Rumblings in pro farmers party BBB signal rift over leadership
item.description:
Rumblings in pro farmers party BBB signal rift over leadership

February 23, 2026

The recent leadership change at farmers’ party BBB has caused a rift in the party, with prominent members lobbying for outgoing housing minister Mona Keijzer instead of Henk Vermeer.

Party co-founder Caroline van der Plas stepped down on Friday, and named Vermeer as her successor.

But in an email to the party management, leaked to the Telegraaf on Friday, senator Robert van Gasteren, caretaker junior defence minister Gijs Tuinman, and former MP Claudia van Zanten expressed their concern about the party’s future.

The trio said BBB co-founder Henk Vermeer is “a respected and valued party member,” but that Mona Keijzer is a “very experienced administrator and politician who will bring in the votes. She has a broad appeal and was mentioned as being prime minister material for a reason,” the e-mail read.

In the last elections, Keijzer gained almost as many preference votes as Van der Plas.

While it is customary for the parliamentary party – now just four MPs – to elect a new leader, “we do not understand or accept that such a momentous decision has not been put before the membership,” the e-mail continued.

The trio also claimed the leadership change is “questioned in all layers of the BBB”. Other prominent BBB members support Vermeer, they told broadcaster NOS.

According to NOS political correspondent Ewoud Kievit, the departure of Caroline van der Plas has caused confusion about the identity of the party.

Vermeer and others in the party management want a return to the origins of the party, following the disappointing election outcome, and to concentrate on farming and the countryside. Team Keijzer, however, is campaigning for a “social conservative people’s party” with space for Keijzer’s anti-migration stance.

Tuinman said in a reaction that the e-mail is part of a lively exchange about where the party is going, its identity and its strategy in the run-up to the final vote for a new leader by the parliamentary party on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Hanneke Sanou

item.title: Opening a Dutch bank account just got a whole lot easier
item.description:
Partner content

Opening a Dutch bank account just got a whole lot easier

February 23, 2026

Traditionally, opening a bank account in the Netherlands was a long, bureaucratic process, a hassle which new arrivals certainly did not need when they had just arrived. But those days are gone. And on top of that, you will now receive a €125 incentive when opening an account at ABN AMRO.

New arrivals to the Netherlands no longer have to cut through metres of red tape before they can open a bank account, pay the deposit on their new home or even use public transport. Internet banking is here to stay, and ABN AMRO, for example, is focusing on offering faster, simpler and more welcoming ways for new international residents to start managing their finances in the Netherlands.

The bank has provided specialised services to international clients for the past 25 years and was previously a ground-breaker in allowing internationals to start banking before they had been given their citizen service number (BSN), an essential document on the road to Dutch residency.

Same day activation

Now the bank has gone one step further, so that newly arrived expats no longer have to prove their Dutch address before getting their money organised. That means EU and EEA clients can now open their accounts online and start using them immediately.

This same day account activation means you can receive payments, transfer money and start managing your finances on the same day as you sign up. Users can also link their new account directly to Apple Pay or Google Pay so they can start paying for goods and services without having to wait for their physical debit card to arrive.

“Our goal is to make financial life in the Netherlands easier for expats — with clear, practical advice you can apply immediately,” says Laura Wetser, ABN AMRO’s head of financial health. This strategy has allowed the bank to take the digital lead without forgetting the human touch – as a high street bank with a state-of-the-art mobile banking app.

A €125 incentive and many more benefits

And if you think banks are all about taking your money and doing little in return, ABN AMRO is offering new clients up to €125 on opening their first account to help them pay for all those little expenses which keep cropping up.

Open an account and receive up to €125.

ABN AMRO account holders also have access to the bank’s English language International Client Desk, where advisers are on hand and ready to answer any questions about savings, investments and pensions in the Netherlands. The service is free of charge, with no strings attached.

If you are planning to move to the Netherlands in the coming months or have recently arrived and want to know more about managing your finances the Dutch way, ABN AMRO is hosting a free seminar on March 23 about keeping fit financially. Sign up here

“Understanding Dutch finances doesn’t have to be difficult. With the right guidance, expats can feel at home here much faster,” says the bank’s head of sales, Harm van de Minkelis.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pascoe

item.title: House prices rise again, but have been levelling for 10 months
item.description:
House prices rise again, but have been levelling for 10 months

February 23, 2026

House prices in the Netherlands reached a record high in January, driven by a continuing shortage of homes, a lack of new-build properties and rising household income, national statistics agency CBS said on Monday.

The average price of an existing owner-occupied home now stands at €493,875, up 5.4% on a year ago, the CBS and the land registry department said. Compared with December, prices rose by 1.2%.

Price rises for existing homes are slowing, however, and have moderated for the 10th month in a row.

“The increase is mainly due to the tight housing market,” CBS economist Marjolijn Jaarsma said. “Demand is high and supply is limited, and that trend is continuing. We see it nationwide.”

New construction is also lagging behind government targets for the third year in a row. Nearly 80,000 homes were completed last year, compared with 82,000 in 2024. The aim is to build 100,000 new homes a year.

“The low level of supply remains a major problem,” ABN Amro economist Jan-Paul van de Kerke told news website Nu.nl. “At the same time, households have more to spend, allowing people to take out larger mortgages. That is fuelling demand on the housing market.”
item.dc:creator: Robin Pascoe

item.title: Amsterdam says 20 million tourist cap is not legally binding
item.description:
Amsterdam says 20 million tourist cap is not legally binding

February 23, 2026

Amsterdam officials have described the limit on tourist numbers – set at 20 million overnight stays in 2020 – as a target rather than a legal threshold in court documents relating to a dispute with local residents, the Parool reported on Monday.

Last year, a group of residents began legal action against the city council, arguing it had failed to take sufficient steps to reduce tourist numbers to below the 20 million limit, which has been exceeded every year since the coronavirus pandemic.

However, in written evidence to the court, officials say the provisions outlined in documents intended to ensure a balanced approach to tourism are a policy directive for internal use only and cannot be enforced.

That means, officials argue, that residents cannot derive any rights from them, including the 20 million overnight stay limit.

The residents, united under the name Amsterdam heeft een keuze, or Amsterdam has a choice, are demanding that tourist stays are reduced to below 20 million by 2028 and remain below that level thereafter. They also want the council to publish the impact of the different measures it has introduced.

The case is being heard in the civil courts in Amsterdam and is currently focused on procedural issues. No date has yet been set for the main hearings.

Amsterdam introduced the limit in 2020 after more than 30,000 residents signed a petition calling for action and pledged to take steps to keep to the maximum.

City tourism chief Sofyan Mbarki – who is leading the PvdA campaign in the local elections – said at the time he understood that residents are unhappy but said that tackling excessive tourism will take time.

The city has already taken steps, such as reducing the options for holiday rentals, increasing tourist tax and launching a “stay away” campaign to discourage budget tourists out to take drugs and party.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pascoe

item.title: NEC draw with Ajax in Arena to keep Champions League hopes alive
item.description:
NEC draw with Ajax in Arena to keep Champions League hopes alive

February 22, 2026

NEC Nijmegen bagged a valuable point in Amsterdam to maintain their narrow advantage in the battle for third place in the Eredivisie.

Ajax went ahead late in a first half in which NEC were the better side, but were punished for a defensive lapse by Kodai Sano that allowed Mika Godts to dribble into the penalty area and score from close range.

The visitors’ enterprise was rewarded in unorthodox fashion in the 57th minute when Darko Nejasmic saw his volley from a corner blocked but was able to turn in the rebound while lying on the ground.

Leaders PSV found themselves 1-0 down at home to Heerenveen after their defeat the previous weekend by Voldendam, but turned the match around in the second half as Ricardo Pepi came off the bench to score.

Lasse Nordas pounced on some sloppy defending by Jerdy Schouten to give Heerenveen the lead in the 21st minute.

Ivan Perisic equalised on the stroke of half-time when his cross from the left flank drifted in before Myron Boadu, making a rare appearance in the starting line-up for PSV, turned in a cross from Paul Wanner four minutes after the restart.

Pepi came on for the last 15 minutes, having recovered from a broken arm sustained six weeks ago, and had a goal disallowed for offside within minutes. But the American was not to be denied as he scored PSV’s third with a curling shot from outside the penalty area in the 88th minute.

Go Ahead Eagles eased their relegation fears with a 4-0 win against Heracles that keeps their opponents at the foot of the table. Three of the goals came in a 15-minute spell in the second half, after Søren Tengstedt had opened the scoring with a deflected shot.

NAC beat Volendam, conquerers of PSV last weekend, with a late goal by 17-year-old striker Pepijn Reulen in his eighth league match to haul themselves within two points of their rivals, who occupy the all-important 15th spot.

Fortuna Sittard pulled away from the drop zone as a penalty by Mohammed Ihattaren and a header from a corner by Kaj Sierhuis were enough to take all three points against Excelsior, who slip to 14th place.

AZ go fifth

AZ recovered from their midweek defeat by Armenian side FC Noah to beat Sparta 3-1, a result that lifts them above the Rotterdam club into fifth place. All four goals came in the last 25 minutes, with Troy Parrott receiving a booking for taking his shirt off to celebrate his 13th goal of the season in the final minute.

Groningen’s slump in form continued as they succumbed to a fifth successive league defeat at Twente, despite taking a first-half lead when Marvin Peersman headed in from a corner.

The scores were level within four minutes when Daan Rots beat goalkeeper Etienne Vaessen to a through ball and steered the ball into the empty net from a narrow angle, before Kristian Hlynsson took advantage of a mistake by Peersman to score the winner.

Utrecht and PEC shared the points as Angel Alarcón gave the home side an early lead and had a chance to secure the points in the second half when he was clear through on goal, but was unable to find a way past Tom de Graaf. PEC equalised a minute later with a tap-in by Thijs Oosting.

Results

Friday

Fortuna Sittard 2-1 Excelsior

Saturday

Ajax 1-1 NEC Nijmegen

NAC Breda 1-0 Volendam

PSV Eindhoven 3-1 Heerenveen

Sunday

AZ Alkmaar 3-1 Sparta Rotterdam

Feyenoord L-L Telstar

Go Ahead Eagles 4-0 Heracles Almelo

Twente Enschede 2-1 Groningen

Utrecht 1-1 PEC Zwolle
item.dc:creator: Gordon Darroch

item.title: More Dutch golds in skating mass start, taking medal total to 20
item.description:
More Dutch golds in skating mass start, taking medal total to 20

February 21, 2026

Marijke Groenewoud and Jorrit Bergsma have added two more gold medals to the Dutch Winter Olympic collection, each taking the top spot in the mass start race.

The gold is 27-year-old Groenewoud’s first individual medal, having won silver in the team pursuit earlier in the Games. She follows Irene Schouten, who won mass start gold at the Beijing Olympics four years ago. Ivanie Blondin of Canada and Mia Manganello took silver and bronze.

In the men’s race, Jorrit Bergsma won gold ahead of Viktor Thorup of Denmark and Andrea Giovannini of Italy, who edged out favourite Jordan Stolz. It is the 40-year-old’s second medal of the Games after he won a surprise bronze in the 10,000 metres.

The Dutch squad has won 20 medals at the Games, including 10 gold, which is a record. The Netherlands is now in third place in the medals table, just ahead of Italy, with one day of competition to go.

Five of the Dutch golds were for long-track speed skating events and five for short-track.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pascoe
